abstract: This article explores the missional role of the Holy Spirit through the lens of Ghanaian Pentecostal theology and practice. Utilizing the concept of Missio Dei—specifically a trinitarian approach to mission—as a point of departure, the study focuses on the indispensable work of the Holy Spirit in the expansion of the church. Central to this discussion are Ghanaian Pentecostal perspectives on the baptism and infilling of the Spirit, alongside the lived ecclesial practices that stem from these beliefs. The article concludes that the church’s vocational success is entirely dependent upon the Holy Spirit, who both convicts the world of sin and empowers believers with the spiritual gifts necessary to boldly proclaim the gospel.
